logs.name: camel-director
logs.text:
> No, but that's only because counterfeiting causes inflation. Which negatively impacts me.

Counterfeiting causes devaluation of money (through inflation).  Pirating causes devaluation of the good (through much the same means - having more of something available decreases its price).  This devaluation of the good negatively impacts the creator.

Why is the former unacceptable to you, but the latter is fine?  If it's just because one negatively impacts you and the other doesn't, then you don't have a real moral argument, you are just being selfish.

logs.name: bitter-photon
logs.text:
>>Morality - stealing is wrong. I don't think this requires much elaboration.

Stealing implies that something is lost. Piracy is a duplication of goods. Companies are not out goods they are selling because of people pirating only money.


>>The fines for illegal downloading can be up to $30,000 per song, or higher at the judge's discretion. You are effectively gambling a year of your life every time you download a song illegally.

The odds of this happening are a like a reverse lottery. Furthermore, a music company  would have to stand to make money by suing you, which they won't.

>>The only real barrier to prosecuting every single internet pirate is the Constitutional right to a jury trial. It is all recorded forever, it is all data, it can all be automated except for the jury trial that they have to give you even though your IP address effectively proves that it was you. You are banking on the government never making an exception to this principle, no matter how much artificial intelligence advances, and even though it would instantly make every major corporation millions and millions of dollars.

This isn't true, the other barrier to entry is the fact that they also have to prove a specific person did something autonomously. If a family member steals my computer and has access to my wifi and illegally downloads something, and courts come after me for it, they still have to prove *I* did it. If they can't they've already lost far more money then they can hope to reclaim on a worthless case. Never mind the cost of litigation itself.  What if I have no money to take?  The company will never recoup costs of 100% of the people they sue, even if they could 1 button click lawsuit everyone, which means the costs they *do recover* likely would be a loss anyway. But that's a technological pipedream anyway. I'd argue that this aspect of your argument, for the most part is extremely tinfoil hat.

logs.name: ebony-equalizer
logs.text: @camel-director, I don't think it's that simple. Just because pirating doesn't directly take money from the creator's pocket, it doesn't mean it's not harming them. And @bitter-photon, I get what you're saying about stealing being wrong, but the whole piracy thing is more complicated than that. What about people who pirate stuff because they can't afford it? Are they just supposed to go without?

logs.name: bitter-photon
logs.text: "@ebony-equalizer, so now you're saying that it's okay to steal if you can't afford something? That's not how it works. If you can't afford a video game or a song, then maybe you should just wait until you can or find alternative free options. Pirating isn't a charity, it's theft. And @camel-director, your whole argument is based on the idea that pirating doesn't hurt anyone, but it does. It hurts the creators who don't get paid for their work. Just because you don't see the direct impact doesn't mean it's not there."

logs.name: happy-watt
logs.text: "@bitter-photon, come on, you're not gonna lecture us on morality, are you? Newsflash: people have been sharing and copying stuff for centuries, it's not like piracy is some new concept. And what about all the free music and movies out there that artists put out themselves? Is that theft too? Get real, dude."

logs.name: knurled-dividend
logs.text: "@happy-watt, yeah I'm with you on this! And @bitter-photon, you're being super strict. I mean, I've downloaded games before that I couldn't afford and it didn't hurt anyone. In fact, it made me like the game so much I bought the next one in the series when I could afford it. It's like free advertising or something!"

logs.name: coal-title
logs.text: "@bitter-photon, I see your point, but sometimes people just can't afford things, no matter how much they want them. I've been in tough spots like that before. @happy-watt and @knurled-dividend have a point too, sharing and copying have been around forever. Maybe instead of calling it theft, we should be looking at why people feel the need to pirate in the first place. Is it really just about the money, or is there something more to it?"

logs.name: camel-director
logs.text: "@coal-title, I think you're hitting the nail on the head. It's not just about the money, it's about accessibility. I've been on camping trips where we've shared stories and music around the campfire, and it's a totally different experience than buying a ticket to a concert. Maybe instead of cracking down on piracy, we should be looking at ways to make content more accessible to people who can't afford it. @bitter-photon, I'm not saying piracy is right, but let's not forget that there are bigger issues at play here."

logs.name: crunchy-tensor
logs.text: "@camel-director, that's a pretty idealistic view, don't you think? I mean, I get what you're saying about accessibility, but at the end of the day, creators need to get paid for their work. If everyone just shares and copies without paying, how are they supposed to make a living? @coal-title, you're right that there are bigger issues at play, but let's not gloss over the fact that piracy is still theft, no matter how you spin it. And @knurled-dividend, just because you liked a game and bought the next one doesn't mean that piracy is okay. What if the devs didn't make enough from the first game to fund the second one because everyone pirated it?"

logs.name: ebony-equalizer
logs.text: "@crunchy-tensor, I get what you're saying about creators needing to get paid, but what about all the big companies that make millions off of their stuff and still charge us an arm and a leg? It's not like they're struggling to make ends meet. And @camel-director has a point about accessibility, maybe if things were more affordable, people wouldn't feel the need to pirate as much. It's all about finding a balance, right?"

logs.name: bitter-photon
logs.text: "@crunchy-tensor, come on, you're acting like every pirated copy is a lost sale. Newsflash: most people who pirate stuff wouldn't have bought it anyway. And @ebony-equalizer is right, the big companies are just price-gouging. I mean, have you seen the prices of some of these concert tickets or video games? It's like they're trying to rob us blind. Maybe instead of whining about piracy, they should focus on making their stuff more affordable"

logs.name: happy-watt
logs.text: "@bitter-photon, that's a pretty weak argument, don't you think? Just because someone wouldn't have bought it anyway doesn't mean it's okay to pirate. And @ebony-equalizer, I'm not talking about the big companies, I'm talking about the indie devs who are actually struggling to make a living. You think they're price-gouging too? Get real. @crunchy-tensor has a point, piracy is still theft, no matter how you spin it."

logs.name: knurled-dividend
logs.text: "@happy-watt, easy for you to say when you're probably one of those people who can afford to buy all the latest games and concert tickets. Not everyone is as lucky as you, and piracy is sometimes the only way for people to access stuff they can't afford. And @bitter-photon has a point, the prices are ridiculous! Maybe if they made it more affordable, people wouldn't feel the need to pirate as much"

logs.name: coal-title
logs.text: "@knurled-dividend, I see what you're saying, but that's like saying it's okay to shoplift because you can't afford something. Piracy is still wrong, even if prices are high. @bitter-photon, I agree with you that prices are outrageous, but maybe instead of pirating, we should be pushing for change in the industry. @happy-watt has a point about the indie devs, they're the ones who get hurt the most by piracy. We should be supporting them, not stealing from them"

logs.name: camel-director
logs.text: "@coal-title, I'm with you on this one. It's not about justifying piracy, it's about finding a solution that works for everyone. And @knurled-dividend, I'm not some rich guy who can afford everything, I'm just someone who believes in paying for the stuff I enjoy. If people can't afford it, maybe they should be looking for free alternatives or waiting for sales, not stealing from the creators. @happy-watt made a good point about the indie devs, they're the backbone of the industry and need our support."

logs.name: crunchy-tensor
logs.text: "@knurled-dividend, come on, that's a pretty weak excuse for piracy. Just because you can't afford something doesn't mean you're entitled to steal it. And @coal-title is right, it's not like shoplifting just because you can't afford a new video game. If you can't afford it, don't buy it, or wait for a sale. It's not that hard. And @camel-director, I'm with you, supporting indie devs is crucial, they're the ones who bring us the most innovative games and music, not some big corporation."

logs.name: ebony-equalizer
logs.text: "@crunchy-tensor, I totally agree with you! It's like, if I really want a new game, I'll save up for it or wait for a sale. I don't just go and download it for free, that's not fair to the devs who worked hard on it. And @coal-title, you made a great point about pushing for change in the industry, maybe we can make a difference if we all speak up!"
